disease.name: Mirizzi Syndrome
disease.note: Rare complication of chronic gallstone disease where a stone impacted in the cystic duct or gallbladder neck causes extrinsic compression of the common hepatic duct, leading to obstructive jaundice. Type I: external compression without fistula. Type II-V: cholecystobiliary fistula with varying degrees of common bile duct erosion. Can mimic cholangiocarcinoma on imaging. Surgical management is challenging with high bile duct injury risk.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: With cholangitis -> EMERGENCY; Type II-V -> URGENT surgical referral; Type I -> ELECTIVE

historyFeatures.note: Chronic recurrent biliary colic; then persistent pain

subtypes.name: Mirizzi Syndrome Type I (Extrinsic Compression)
subtypes.management: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with careful dissection. High conversion rate to open. Intraoperative cholangiogram essential. Partial cholecystectomy if dense inflammation.

subtypes.name: Mirizzi Syndrome Type II-V (With Cholecystobiliary Fistula)
subtypes.management: Open approach recommended. Cholecystectomy + fistula repair. Type II: primary repair of CBD defect over T-tube. Type III: Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. Type IV: hepaticojejunostomy. Type V: hepaticojejunostomy + enterolithotomy.

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin)
laboratory.indication: obstructive pattern: elevated conjugated bilirubin, ALP, GGT

laboratory.name: CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: may be mildly elevated; not reliable to distinguish from malignancy

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: gallbladder stones; contracted gallbladder; dilated intrahepatic ducts; narrowed CHD; impacted stone in gallbladder neck

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.indication: non-invasive: extrinsic compression of CHD by impacted cystic duct stone; may show fistula; distinguishes from cholangiocarcinoma

imaging.name: ERCP
imaging.indication: therapeutic and diagnostic: stent placement to relieve jaundice before surgery; identifies fistula; can place plastic stent

imaging.name: CT abdomen
imaging.indication: gallstone in cystic duct/neck; dilated IHBD; normal CBD caliber; rule out mass

management.protocolReference: ASGE Guidelines for Mirizzi Syndrome; SAGES Guidelines

pathways.criteria: Extrinsic compression of CHD by stone in cystic duct/gallbladder neck; no fistula
pathways.treatment: Cholecystectomy (laparoscopic with low threshold for conversion). Intraoperative cholangiogram essential. Partial cholecystectomy if Calot's triangle is hostile (dense inflammation). Preoperative ERCP with stent if jaundice severe or cholangitis. Postoperative: T-tube if CHD injury suspected.

pathways.criteria: Cholecystobiliary fistula present, type determined by degree of CBD erosion
pathways.treatment: Open cholecystectomy preferred. Fistula repair: Type II (erosion <33% CBD) -> primary closure over T-tube. Type III (33-50%) -> Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. Type IV (complete CBD destruction) -> hepaticojejunostomy. Type V (fistula + gallstone ileus) -> hepaticojejunostomy + enterolithotomy. Preoperative ERCP with stent to define anatomy and relieve jaundice. Postoperative: drain; antibiotics; monitor for bile leak.
